issues.description: All controllers use console.log(error) for error handling instead of structured logging. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but does not provide sufficient context for security monitoring or incident response.
issues.suggestion: Consider adopting a structured logging library (e.g., Winston, Pino) that captures request context, user identity, and error details in a machine-parseable format.

issues.description: The organization creation endpoint (POST /organizations) allows any authenticated member to create a new tenant organization. The documentation mentions authorization enforcement but there is no visible guard or role check at the controller level beyond MemberAuth. Creating organizations is a privileged operation that should be restricted to platform admins.
issues.suggestion: Add an explicit role-based guard (e.g., @Roles('admin') or a platform-level capability check) to the create endpoint to ensure only authorized actors can create new tenant organizations.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to any of these endpoints. Task creation, update, and deletion endpoints could be abused by automated clients.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the API gateway or using a NestJS throttler guard (e.g., @nestjs/throttler) to protect sensitive mutation endpoints.

issues.description: The guest list endpoint (`GET /erpHrmTimeTracking/guest/guests`) exposes a paginated list of all guest identity records (email, id, timestamps) to any guest actor. Unauthenticated visitors should not be able to enumerate all guest emails in the system. This enables user enumeration and privacy leakage.
issues.suggestion: Restrict this endpoint to admin/member actors only, or remove it entirely if there is no legitimate use case for guests to list other guest identities. If it must remain, ensure it returns only the caller's own record.

issues.description: The guest session upsert endpoint (`PATCH /erpHrmTimeTracking/guest/guests`) accepts `ip`, `href`, and `referrer` values directly from the client request body and persists them as-is for 'auditability'. Client-supplied IP addresses are not trustworthy and can be spoofed. The real IP should be extracted from the request context (e.g., `@Ip()` decorator or trusted proxy headers), not from the request body.
issues.suggestion: Remove `ip` from the request body DTO. Extract the client IP server-side using NestJS `@Ip()` or from trusted `X-Forwarded-For` headers configured at the infrastructure level. Only `href` and `referrer` (which are inherently client-supplied) should come from the body, and they should be validated/sanitized.

issues.description: The report generation endpoint accepts the full `IErpHrmTimeTrackingReportDefinition` object as the request body rather than just a report definition ID and generation parameters. Accepting the entire definition object from the client could allow a caller to supply arbitrary report definition data (dimensions, filters, organization scoping) rather than referencing a server-side persisted definition, potentially bypassing organization-scoping controls.
issues.suggestion: The request body should accept only a report definition identifier (UUID) plus generation-specific parameters (date range, overrides). The report definition itself should be loaded server-side by ID, ensuring it belongs to the caller's organization. Do not trust client-supplied definition structures for security-sensitive scoping.

issues.description: All controllers use `console.log(error)` for error handling, which may leak stack traces or sensitive data to logs and provides no structured audit trail. This is the standard AutoBE pattern but worth noting once.
issues.suggestion: Replace `console.log(error)` with a structured logger (e.g., NestJS Logger) that supports log levels and redacts sensitive fields. Avoid logging full error objects that may contain PII or credentials.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to authentication endpoints (join, login, refresh). These endpoints are prime targets for brute-force and credential stuffing attacks.
issues.suggestion: Apply rate limiting at the infrastructure level (e.g., API gateway, reverse proxy) or use a NestJS throttling guard (e.g., `@nestjs/throttler`) on authentication endpoints, especially login.

issues.description: Password complexity validation beyond basic type checking is not visible in the generated code. The join endpoint should enforce minimum password strength requirements.
issues.suggestion: Add password complexity validation in the provider layer or via a custom Typia/class-validator constraint: minimum length, mixed case, numbers, and special characters.

issues.description: The query selects `password_hash` field for employee records in the report generation run output. This sensitive credential data is fetched from the database and potentially included in the API response, exposing password hashes to API consumers.
issues.suggestion: Remove `password_hash: true` from all employee select clauses. Password hashes must never be included in query projections used for API responses.

issues.description: The delete operation performs no authorization check. Any authenticated (or unauthenticated) caller can delete any report generation run by ID. There is no tenant/organization scope validation to ensure the caller owns the resource.
issues.suggestion: Before deleting, verify that the report generation run belongs to the caller's organization by joining through the report definition's organization_id and comparing it to the authenticated member's organization context.

issues.description: Report generation run creation has no authorization check. The provider accepts a `reportDefinitionId` and creates a run without verifying that the caller belongs to the organization that owns the report definition. Any user can trigger report generation for any organization's report definition.
issues.suggestion: Validate that the report definition's `erp_hrm_time_tracking_organization_id` matches the authenticated member's selected organization context before creating the run.

issues.description: The endpoint retrieves a report output metric by ID with no organization scoping or authorization check. Any authenticated user can access any metric record by guessing or knowing its UUID.
issues.suggestion: Add organization scope validation by joining through the report output and report generation run to the report definition's organization_id, and verify it matches the caller's organization context.

issues.description: Department retrieval has no organization scope check. The query fetches a department by ID without verifying that the department belongs to the authenticated member's organization, enabling cross-tenant data access.

issues.description: Department update fetches the department by ID only, without verifying the department belongs to the authenticated member's organization. An attacker can update departments in other organizations.

issues.description: The delete activity log entry function is a no-op stub that returns immediately without performing any deletion or authorization check. This means the endpoint silently succeeds for any input, which could mask security issues and represents a broken access control implementation.
issues.suggestion: Implement the actual deletion logic with proper authorization: verify the log entry belongs to the authenticated member's organization and that the member has permission to delete it, then perform the actual database deletion.

issues.description: The organization_id is retrieved using `(props.member as any).organization_id`, bypassing TypeScript type safety. If the MemberPayload type does not include organization_id, this cast could silently produce undefined, leading to incorrect organization scoping.
issues.suggestion: Ensure `organization_id` is a typed field on `MemberPayload` rather than using an unsafe `any` cast. Validate that the organization_id is present before using it.

issues.description: The code uses a non-null assertion `lock!` after checking `if (!lock)` which throws via findUniqueOrThrow. The logic is convoluted: if lock is null, it calls findUniqueOrThrow (which will throw), but then continues with `lock!` assertions. This pattern is fragile and could lead to unexpected behavior if the control flow changes.
issues.suggestion: Simplify by using `findUniqueOrThrow` directly in the initial query, removing the redundant null check and non-null assertions.

issues.description: The project update provider does not perform any database operations. It constructs a fake response object from the request body without validating ownership, persisting changes, or checking that the project belongs to the authenticated member's organization. This is a stub that silently accepts any input.
issues.suggestion: Implement actual database update logic with proper authorization: verify the project belongs to the member's organization and that the member has permission to update it.

issues.description: The create project provider does not actually create a project. It queries session data and returns it cast as a project, which is a stub implementation. No authorization or business logic is enforced.
issues.suggestion: Implement actual project creation with proper organization scoping, authorization checks, and database persistence.

issues.description: The catch block in guestAuthorize swallows all exceptions from the database lookup and throws a generic UnauthorizedException. This means database errors (connection failures, etc.) are indistinguishable from invalid sessions, potentially masking infrastructure issues. Additionally, a valid JWT with a non-existent session_id in the database throws ForbiddenException inside the try block but is caught and re-thrown as UnauthorizedException, changing the error semantics.
issues.suggestion: Only catch specific exceptions related to invalid sessions. Let database/infrastructure errors propagate or be handled separately. The ForbiddenException thrown inside the try block should not be caught and converted to UnauthorizedException.

issues.description: The controller has `@x-autobe-authorization-type null` and `@x-autobe-authorization-actor null` on all endpoints (POST, PATCH, GET, PUT, DELETE), indicating no authentication guards are applied. These are write operations (create, update, delete) on report definition filters that should require authentication.
issues.suggestion: Apply appropriate NestJS Guards to enforce authentication and authorization on all mutating endpoints (POST, PATCH, PUT, DELETE). Read endpoints may be acceptable without guards depending on data sensitivity.

issues.description: All controller methods use `console.log(error)` for error handling, which is the standard AutoBE pattern but lacks structured logging with request context, user identity, and severity levels.
issues.suggestion: Consider implementing a structured logging solution (e.g., Winston, Pino) that captures request context, user identity, and error details for better observability and security monitoring.

issues.description: No rate limiting is applied to any endpoints in this controller, including write operations that modify report configuration.

issues.description: The join/registration endpoint is a stub that throws 501. Once implemented, ensure password hashing, input validation, and audit logging are included.

agentEvaluations.summary: [sampled 30/172 files] [4 chunks, 27→26 issues] The controllers follow standard AutoBE patterns with MemberAuth decorators applied consistently across all endpoints, providing baseline authentication. Authorization logic is delegated to provider functions, which is acceptable but makes it difficult to verify access control correctness at the controller layer. The main concerns are: (1) organization creation and deletion are highly privileged operations with no visible elevated-role guard at the controller level; (2) console.log error handling lacks structured context; (3) no rate limiting is present. No injection, cryptographic, or SSRF vulnerabilities are visible in the controller layer. Overall security posture is reasonable for an AutoBE-generated codebase.
